speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's May the 16th, 1995. The survivor is Jules Fischer Forgacs. The interviewer is Louise McLeod. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. The interview is in English. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Louise McLeod. Today's date is Tuesday, the 16th of May, 1995. I'm conducting the interview with Jules Forgacs. And it's being conducted in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is spelled L-O-U-I-S-E, Louise; and McLeod, M-C-L-E-O-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's rolling. And the camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name and spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jules Forgacs, J-U-L-E-S, F-O-R-G-A-C-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your name at birth and spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius, J-U-L-I-U-S, Fischer, F-I-S-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nicknames? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 3rd of May, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 66 last week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me the city-- your city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same. 9-- 3rd of May, 1929, in Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the suburbs of Budapest, right in the middle of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Garay ter. That's had a market where my mother had a shop for 60 odd years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Louise, could you just go from the top again? I just-- the camera had a little-- little interruption. OK. Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the whole beginning?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the beginning? Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell me your name, and could you spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-U-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, no. Just from family life and that sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Can we start?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you describe your home and family?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We lived in-- in Garay ter, number 15. And we lived together-- my father, my mother, my sister, and my grandmother, who was the mother of my father. We lived in a-- a two-room apartment. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father and my mother lived in the bedroom, and my grandmother, my sister, and myself, we lived in the living room, where it was removable beds. And-- and that's how I remember beginning my life. We had a-- a-- a family-- quite a large one. My grandmother had four sisters-- four daughters and one-- and two son. One son died just exactly 10 years before my birth, in very young age. He was-- he met an accident where a telegram post hit his head, and he died instantly. And I born exactly on the same day, 10 years after. My sister is two years-- two years older than I am. And we had-- because my parents had a business, a-- a little poultry shop down in the market, we seen very little of the parents. My mother went away 4 o'clock in the morning and came home at 6:00, 7:00 in the evening. And my father did the same. My grandmother stayed home. And we had a-- a-- you could call a servant, who looked after. And they more or less raised us. And I-- I went to school in the usual way, the-- the usual four years of elementary school, and-- and four years of-- of middle class, what we called. And before the war, I enrolled two years on the Jewish technical college, we can call it. And after the war, I finished another two years. So I finished, more or less, a technical college course. And before the war, our religious life was my father was involved in the Jewish community, and he was also involved in-- in her-- in his profession. He was the president of the Hungarian Poultry Association, if you could call it. The translation is more or less like that. And we were a-- a middle class family. My father was well known in the area. And to me, as a child, I-- I grew up with-- with football, actually, soccer. But I call it football. And that was our main entertainment. After school, we went to the park and played till 6 o'clock, 7 o'clock in the evening. Now, the family, I had a lot of first cousins, because of the-- of that three daughters of-- of my grandmother, which means my father had three sisters. And-- and my grandfather died very, very young age. So my father raised the-- the-- himself and the three sisters, plus my grandmother. And he was the breadwinner. So he didn't go much to school. And financially, we were quite well off. I never knew anything which I wanted I wouldn't get. I mean, those days, the values were different of today's. If you had a-- a-- a little football with your grandmother's stockings and newspaper inside, that-- that was already a-- a wealth. And-- and that's how we lived up. We went to the swimming pools. In the Saturdays, Sundays, we went to the football match. And-- and up till 1940, '41, '42, we didn't know much about the war, because Hungary, it seems, was spared of-- of the misery of-- of the war. When-- my first knowledge in school was that they called me a Jew-- not even bloody Jew, just Jew. But I could whack him in the nose, because nobody ask me why. Or when we had a fight, I was the Englishman, they were the Germans, and-- and we lived happily ever after. So-- but till 19-- 1944, March, when the Germans came in, that's when the-- the persecution started, in my eyes, as a young boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about the Jewish community in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community meant that most of my parents' friends were also down from the business side, and about 80% of them were Jews. We all attended to what we call the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] Synagogue. That's where I got bar mitzvah. And-- but we were not a tremendously religious Jews. And our religious upbringing was that the parents were in the synagogue, and we played soccer in the background-- in the back garden of the-- of the synagogue. And my father kept a friendship with a lot of non-Jews. And-- and that's-- that's about I can say about our Jewish upbringing. We were very God-fearing. My parents, I don't think I ever-- ever heard a rude word said in front of me, since I can remember. And they've been very decent, hardworking people. And that was our [LAUGHS] contribution to God. Try not to-- not to take advantage of other people. Just live and let live. And that-- that made our [LAUGHS] Jewish conscience clean or clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Hungarian. Although my mother, born in Slovakia, Czechoslovakia, in Trencin, [BACKROUND NOISE] we could not speak any other languages. She-- we went to Slovakia in 1938 or '39 to visit her side of the family. And although, that family, nobody survived. So I seen them only once in my life, and I have vivid memories about them. And when we left Czechoslovakia, that visit after that, I never see them anymore. And-- and we heard during the war that some other atrocity has been done in that part of the world. And unfortunately, after the war, none of them appeared anymore. So we-- we presumed that everybody's dead on-- on my mother's side. My father's side, we had quite a large family, having four-- three sisters, and-- and him. Everybody had two-- every sister had two children. So it was about eight cousins. And-- and we kept, more or less-- my father kept the whole family as like a-- like a hen of the little chickens around him. And so the whole family consisted about 60, 70 people. Unfortunately, out of the 70 people, I was the only one who-- who ventured out of Budapest during the-- the persecution time. And they caught me. And that's how I got to Auschwitz. So-- and my rest of my family went through the war time in Budapest, untouched.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to tell me earlier about your time as being a Jew at school. Could you describe the relationships between Jews and non-Jews in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, because I was [SIGHS] a-- a-- a sport-minded person, our-- our relationship was more a cordial, because I mixed with non-Jews very freely, and they accepted me-- what I was and-- and whoever I was. My recollection of-- of persecution in my childhood was very limited. You know, I would lie if I would say that I had been harassed or-- or-- or been make too much fun of it. I don't think so. I-- they knew if they call me bloody Jew, I-- I whack them in the nose immediately. And because of that, they didn't. That-- to me, the persecution started in 19-- 1944, 3rd of July, when the Hungarian gendarmes-- gendarmes took me and-- and about 40 or 50-- not friend, but same age like me, into a-- a-- a brick work, where they concentrated the people. They collected all the people from around the areas. And in two days' time, they put us into freight trains. And they shipped us to Auschwitz. So that's when-- when my first account started with-- with the anti-Jewish sentiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism experienced by your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I heard, because, as I said, we did not meet too often with our parents. I'm talking about a sister and my-- and-- and myself, because they had been businesspeople. But we knew that it was the numerous new rules in Hungary, which meant that they-- they couldn't-- Jews couldn't hold any official position. They couldn't be in certain places. But otherwise, me, as a-- as a 10 years, 9 years, 6 years, 11-years-old, did not meant too much. My father and my mother, being they owned business, they did-- did not rely on-- on government help with jobs or so forth. So we more or less lived a-- a little bit of a isolated life, away from the every day nastiness. And as I said, myself, I didn't encounter too much until I become 13 and 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your experience at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, I wasn't the greatest of the students. I wasn't the worst either. But I was a good good sportsman. I boxed. I gymnast. I-- I did basketball. I swam. I didn't know I going to be a footballer in that time. But-- but soccer was the-- the sport in-- in-- in Hungary. And study-wise, we had-- [LAUGHS] I know the nastiness started because we had a-- a-- a gymnastic teacher, or what we called PH-- PE-- PE teacher-- who came to the classes with a jackboots and the army uniform. And he was nasty. But those-- those days, I-- I-- I didn't notice that he was only nasty towards Jews. He was nasty to anybody. And he made us to clean his boots, and so forth. And sometimes he might call Jew boy. I-- I don't know. I would lie if I could remember that well that-- by what name he called me. And [LAUGHS] I has a funny experience, because he knew that my parents has poultry shop. So he always ask me, any-- any good stuff came into the shop. And I said, yes. And then he said, OK. Go and bring me some. So I could skip out-- classes, which didn't [? convient ?] me. And he was-- he didn't pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know whether my parents bought his-- his manner towards me better than towards somebody else. But he was hitting us, and-- and whatever those days the school teacher was doing. But later on, he turned to be-- and I heard that-- that they killed him during the war, because he became one of the nastiest, one of the most vicious anti-Semite in-- in our neighborhood. Were there any attempts made by your family to leave Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was made when I was very young. And I only heard from my father that we had a-- a-- a close relatives in Hollywood. She was a very famous Hollywood actress. Her name was Gaal Franciska. [BACKROUND NOISE] And my-- my family got in touch with her. And from America, they provided us with tickets and ship-- shipping tickets. And everything was prepared for the whole family to leave for America. And the last minute, my grandmother said she can't sleep on anybody else's bed but her own. And because of that, [LAUGHS] the whole family-- and this sounds silly-- but the grandmother was the grandmother. She was the-- the elder in the family. So whatever she said, that-- that was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents cancelled, and the whole family stayed. And that could have been somewhere about '37, '38. I-- I couldn't tell exactly. But I knew that the whole family was just about winding up whatever value we had in Hungary and where we intended to leave to-- for the state. But because my grandmother unwillingness, the whole family just stayed there, and that was it. So what were their reasons for wanting to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I wouldn't know. I was very young. They've-- they never discussed it with me. So I wouldn't be able to answer you that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the rise of Nazism perceived in your family and community?

speaker: interviewee
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: The rise.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rise of Nazism. In 1942, we already noticed that something is going on around the world, because the Jewish welfare agency in Budapest was notifying Jewish families to receive young children who gathered in Budapest from the neighboring countries, like Slovakia, Romania, and so forth. And they had been dropped over the border by certain organizations, because their parents have been already taken to concentration camps. And we invited, through the Hungarian agencies, these children, for Sunday, Saturday, or certain days, to have lunch and dinner at our place. And these-- these children, being very young, they also didn't know much about the happenings. But they knew their parents disappeared. Their hair was shaven, which means their head was shaven. They had no hair. And-- and as a youngsters, we looked at amazement to what is going on. And my parents were aware of it, definitely. Because when the-- for the-- in front of the children, it wasn't much said. But when adults came to our home, and-- and we were-- had to go to the kitchen while the adults was talking, we heard all the-- all the stories-- what's going on-- but being very young, couldn't comprehend or-- or wouldn't sink in, actually, what is happening. We knew. We read a little bit of newspaper. Not because there was no newspaper, but because we read very little newspaper. We knew that what's happening with Hitler coming on power and-- and-- [BACKROUND NOISE] and what happened in Italy, and-- and Mussolini came to power. And we heard of the Princess of Windsor, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So news was coming in. And in the early-- late '30s, very early '40s, we were still very well informed about the happening in-- in-- in Europe. But as I said in my earlier statement, that I, as a child, did not notice too much, which would affect my life tremendously. Amongst your friends, or at school, was there any discussion about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But we played games. And in the games, I was always the Englishman, and the non-Jews were the Germans. [LAUGHS] So we fight like hell. And whoever won the fight won the war. And that was-- that-- that's the most we knew about the-- about about [LAUGHS] the world happenings. We did not-- we did not discuss. We had youngsters who-- who had anti-Semitic tendencies. But fortunately, his mouth was shut in very early times. So I can't say that I had too many bad memories about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you identify as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Identify.

speaker: interviewee
text: Identify a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see what you mean. How do I feel as a Jew? I-- [SIGHS] I-- at the moment? Or on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before--

speaker: interviewer
text: In those times.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-- yeah-- the-- the area we're talking about. It-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew I'm a Jew, because I went to synagogues. And Friday nights, we went to synagogues. And Saturday mornings, we went to synagogues. I was bar mitzvah. I got friendly mostly with Jewish boys, because our neighborhood was Jew. But I-- I knew a lot of people non-Jewish. Because the business of my-- my parents, [BACKROUND NOISE] in that-- that market, it was about 60%, 70% Jews there, and the rest of them was non-Jews. And I knew them exactly the same way. And about my own Judaism is that we got together in the synagogue. And-- and I know my Jewish identities. But I-- I did not consciously made any-- any discovery that I am a Jew. I knew a Jew-- I was a Jew since-- since my first memories came. But it's not because something happened to me-- because the-- the environment I grown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your goals as a young person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SIGHS] It's very difficult. When I went to a football match, I wanted to be a-- a-- a soccer player. When I went to a-- a boxing match, I wanted to be a boxer. And when I went to a basketball game, I wanted to be a basketballers. In those days, our view on life wasn't as wide as the generation today. We were not as educated and not as widely-- we didn't have as wide knowledge about the world. We had more knowledge about the street. We were more streetwise than the children today. But we-- we didn't have to-- we didn't had to be sheltered from the outside life. And because of that, we were too busy to living our childhood. We didn't thought much about what we going to be. We didn't have an aim, because those days, nobody had an-- an aim. It-- it wasn't a life as advanced as today. You could not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] plan to the future so far, because happenings in the world started getting so fast, and-- and-- and positions, and-- and-- and things change so quickly in political ways. But in another way, a family, they're born in the same place. They lived in the same place. And-- and in my eyes, they died in the same place. So it was no-- no-- it was no wandering Jews in my time, that we went from suburbs to suburbs. My grandmother lived in the place where we lived, my father lived. And I thought I'm going to live in the same place when I grow old. And if-- if I am a-- won't-- won't be able enough to-- to start some-- some diplomatic-- I'm sorry-- not diplomatic-- yeah-- diplomatic career-- by that, I mean, if I don't have a diploma of some sort of, I'm going to take over my father's or my mother's business, and-- and live happily ever after. The future wasn't a-- a great importance to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Could you tell me what the first signs of danger were?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first sign of danger-- the first time of danger started to me in-- in 1944, after the Germans came in. That I'm not very familiar with the exact dates, but I'm familiar with exact times, and that's two different things. I can't tell you exactly the day when things happened, but I can tell you about, in my age, what time that represented, that-- that area. And to me, the first sign were that we had to start wearing the Jewish star. Then my parents had to give up the shops, because they couldn't have a shop in the market. They could have a shop outside of the market. They could start their own private-- they can hire or rent a-- a place. And later on, we had to move from our home to a Jewish house. And that's where we moved together with other families, and-- and-- and then we started being cramped up-- two, three families in-- in a two bedrooms home. And because my parents had a poultry shop, food-wise, I didn't know hunger. But I know that on the street, they started looking the Jews with the-- with the David stars, and I had to wear one. And actually, that contributed to my deportation to Germany, because the Jewish welfare agency ask for volunteers to work outside. Because a lot of air raids was on Budapest. And after the air raids, you had to clean up the-- the rubbles and whatever, the air-- whatever damage the air raids caused. And they asked volunteers, young people. And-- and they gave us a-- a-- a-- a pass, which made me eligible to go out from my environment and move freely, which my parents couldn't. And nobody else of the Jewish could. So that's was the one reason I-- I went out to-- to work in this voluntary basis to clean up factories and build some roads which was destroyed by the bombardment. And that's where they caught me. And they put me on the way to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me some more about the German occupation and when it first happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans came in, and I remember so vividly. I-- I was standing in the window and looking down the market, because we lived just next to the-- next to the market. We lived in the first floor. And I looked down, and I see people coming and going in a much larger number that what we usually saw. And then my parents came up. And they said the news just came through that the Germans invaded Hungary. And I was standing there. And-- and a-- a strange feeling came on me, like-- I hope the word is correct-- like a intuition-- that there is something going to happen to me, or-- or this occupation is going to affect me some-- some way. And-- and I'm not a religious Jew, but a very gut-feeling, and very gut-believing guy. And from then on-- it-- it sounds stupid. But whatever happened to me in my life, it happened that I got-- not a sign from God, but I got something telling me to do something which I wouldn't do it ordinarily, which led my life from that moment on, right up today. And that means my deportation to concentration camp, to coming home, to start playing football, to leave Hungary, to leave to Australia, to start a business here, to sell business here. I always got something from up there, which-- which didn't say to me with words, but said to me with something that you do this now, because that's what-- what you have to do. And-- and whatever happened to me in the past, and whatever going to happen me in the future, I do-- when I have this-- [LAUGHS] this signal. And in good Jewish custom-- touch wood-- it never let me down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel-- did people around you or your family feel that Hungary was going to be invaded-- that it would happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We knew. We knew, because the war was on. We knew the war was going on. What my parents knew, I don't know. Because those days, parents does not [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] became involved with-- with their children in-- in anything. The child was a child, and the parents was a parents. If I put the spoon into my mouth at lunchtime before my father did, I got a smack in the nose. Then I went into the corner. Because my upbringing was that adult cames. And when the adult spoke, a child was quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever happened in their life, they tried to shelter it from us. They did not converse about it with us. They didn't give any opinion about it to us. And we were a-- a child who-- who is supposed to be protected from anything which is happening outside. How old were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 13-- 12, 13, 14-- going into-- going into 13. When the Germans came in, I was 13, just after my bar mitzvah. And-- and I got deported from-- from Budapest when I was 13 and 1/2, 14, which mean it's '44, July. I was 14, yeah. Yeah, because I born in '29-- 14 going into 15. And my birthday in May, so I was 14. No, sorry. I was 15 in-- in-- in May, and they took me in July, 1944. So it's makes me 15-years-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape one of the interview with Jules Forgacs. It's the 16th of May, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
